thread.subject: made Strive Soda something
thread.body_text:
Christopher, saw strive's angle pairs a quarterly giveaway with 15% off to hook first orders, curious how that's been converting.

 separate note: I made Strive Soda a 4-min ad fatigue report from your live meta ads. the one about to die, the duplicate eating budget, which to scale, and a fresh angle to test.

 most brands in your niche usually run the same handful of ads too long. this spots it before ROAS drops.

 want it?

 Kyle H.
 Co-founder | OmniRocket

 P.S. if Jack's the better person, point me there :)

Kyle - I get 10 of these emails a day so please send me whatever you think is valuable for Strive and I’ll take a look.
 

 - CC
 CHRIS CLARK, MBA
 Head Of Marketing 
 +1-925-683-9911 
 chris@strivesoda.com 
 Website   |   Instagram  |  LinkedIn

 it flags the ad about to stop working, the near-duplicate splitting your budget, the one to put more behind, and an angle you're not running yet.

 the fixes are quick but easier to just walk you through - 30 min? → Calendar 

 - Kyle

Awesome - looking forward to our call on Monday. 

 Have a great weekend.

 Best,
 Kyle

copy.tldr: Strive is a San Diego hydration soda brand ($32.99 per 12-pack, honey-sweetened, electrolytes) whose Meta ads have near-duplicates splitting budget and a message that fights their own site - our 30 day play is to test three buyer angles cleanly and find the winner before spend gets wasted.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A better-for-you soda brand out of San Diego. They call it 'hydration soda' - a sparkling drink with electrolytes and organic honey. Founded by Jack Espy. Your contact is Chris Clark, Head of Marketing. They sell direct to shoppers online in 12-packs.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Three products, all $32.99 for a 12-pack. Peach Perfect (peach + citrus), Tropical Recharge (passionfruit + coconut), and a Variety Pack with 6 of each. Every can has 180mg electrolytes, is sweetened with organic wildflower honey, and is 45 calories. They also push a Subscribe & Save first month at $16.50.
deep_dive.positioning: Premium and playful. Flavor first, hydration second. They are clear they are NOT a gym drink and NOT a sugar bomb. The tagline is 'Soda That Tastes Amazing. Hydration Built In.'
deep_dive.who_buys: Active, on-the-go adults who love soda taste but want cleaner ingredients. People who will pay a premium to enjoy soda without guilt.
strengths: Clear, differentiated hook - 'hydration soda' is a fresh idea and the honey angle is a real point of difference from Olipop, Poppi, and zero-sugar seltzers.
The Variety Pack has 14 reviews, the most of any product, which suggests it is their easiest first purchase because people want to try both flavors before committing.
gaps: Their live Meta ads say '0 sugar, 0 calories' but the site clearly says 45 calories and honey-sweetened. That is a contradiction that will confuse buyers and could get flagged. The ads are fighting the brand.

Almost no social proof. Two reviews on Peach, zero on Tropical, 14 on the Variety Pack. The site claims '10,000+ Happy Customers' but there is nothing visible to back it up, which makes cold traffic harder to convert.

game_plan.headline: Clean up the duplicate ads, fix the message that fights their site, then test three buyer angles against the Variety Pack to find one winner in 30 days.

phases.goal: Find out which buyer angle and which audience actually works. Not scaling. We want one clear winning angle and one clear winning audience by day 30, plus a message that matches their real product.
campaigns: One testing campaign with three ad sets, one per avatar angle below. Same budget each, so we compare fairly.
A small retargeting campaign for people who visited the site or added to cart but did not buy, since cold traffic on a $32.99 first order rarely buys on the first click.
lead_products: Variety Pack (12 Pack) - lead with this. It has 14 reviews versus 2 and 0 on the single flavors, which tells me it is the easiest first purchase. New buyers do not want to commit to one flavor of a brand they have never tried. Let them try both, then win the repeat order on subscription.
phases.creative: First, kill the near-duplicate ads splitting budget (the two 'Zero Sugar' ads and the two 'Why everyone is reaching for Strive' ads are basically the same). Second, fix the '0 sugar 0 calories' claim - it does not match the 45-calorie honey-sweetened product and it undercuts the honey story that makes them different. Then run three fresh angles, one per avatar: (1) the taste-without-guilt angle, (2) the all-day hydration habit angle, (3) the clean-swap-from-normal-soda angle. Each angle gets a simple video or image plus 2-3 variations. Follow the brand voice: playful, 'vacation in a can', 'slice of paradise'. Stay off the banned claims - no immunity, no detox, no 'cures dehydration', no 'low sugar' as a headline.
phases.budget: Start at $150 a day for the cold testing campaign, split evenly across the three angles ($50 each). Add roughly $30 a day for retargeting. That is about $180 a day, or roughly $5,400 across 30 days. This is a learning budget, not a scaling budget.
phases.what_success_looks_like: By day 30 we know which of the three angles gets the cheapest, most consistent sales and which audience responds. We have a message that matches the real product. We have a first read on whether a $32.99 pack can be sold profitably on cold traffic, and whether the subscription offer helps close the gap.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.value: $150 a day cold, plus about $30 a day retargeting
estimates.basis: $150 a day is enough to get roughly 30 to 40 clicks a day at typical costs in this category, which is the floor for learning which of three angles works before the data is too thin to trust.

estimates.label: Order value we planned around
estimates.basis: All three products are priced at $32.99, so we used that as the stand-in for average order value. Real average order value could be higher if people buy more than one pack or take the subscription.

estimates.label: What 30 days buys
estimates.value: A proven winning angle and audience, not a revenue number
estimates.basis: We have no access to their ad account, Shopify, or analytics, so we cannot honestly promise a revenue figure. Thirty days at this budget is enough to learn what works, which is what the report promised. Scaling and revenue targets come after we see real numbers.

assumptions: The Variety Pack is their bestseller. I am inferring this from review count (14 vs 2 vs 0), not from sales data. If Chris says a single flavor sells better, we lead with that instead.
They are already spending something on Meta, since we can see 5 live ads. If those ads are barely funded or just launched, the cleanup advice still holds but the numbers shift.
Their margin on a $32.99 pack can support paid ads. At about $2.75 per can this is a premium price, which is good, but we need their landed cost to know the real room.
The '0 sugar 0 calories' ad claim is a mistake or leftover, not a different product. We should confirm before touching it.

questions.question: What are you spending on Meta right now, and roughly what does a sale cost you today?
questions.listening_for: A real daily number. If they are spending well under $100 a day, testing three angles fairly will be slow. If they do not know their cost per sale, that is a gap we can help with.

questions.question: Who is running the ads today - you, someone in-house, or another agency?
questions.listening_for: If it is Chris himself, he is stretched thin and wants to hand it off. If it is another agency, find out what is missing. If in-house, who owns it and can they move fast on creative.

questions.question: What is your cost to make and ship one 12-pack? Roughly what is left after that on a $32.99 order?
questions.listening_for: This decides everything. A $32.99 pack needs healthy margin to carry paid ads. If most of the $32.99 is cost, cold traffic will be very hard and subscription becomes the whole game.

questions.question: Last time you scaled ad spend, what happened?
questions.listening_for: Did sales grow with spend or did cost per sale blow up? Tells us if the problem is creative, offer, or the product being a hard sell cold.

questions.question: Your live ads say '0 sugar, 0 calories' but the site says 45 calories and honey-sweetened. Which is right, and why are the ads saying that?
questions.listening_for: This is the biggest thing I spotted. If it is a leftover or an error, we fix it fast. If it is intentional, we need to understand why they are hiding the honey story that makes them different.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site cold, what share of them actually buy? And how many of those come back for a second order?
questions.listening_for: Low first-order conversion points to the price or missing reviews. Low repeat points to the product or flavor. Both change where we spend.

questions.question: How is the Subscribe & Save at $16.50 first month converting? Is it pulling people in or scaring them off with the commitment?
questions.listening_for: The subscription may be their real profit engine. If it converts well, we lean the ads on it. If people bounce at the recurring-charge language, that is a funnel fix.

questions.question: You claim 10,000 happy customers but I see almost no reviews on the site. Where are your reviews living, if anywhere?
questions.listening_for: If they have reviews off-site (Amazon, email, retail), we can pull them onto the site and into ads. If there really are none, we have a social proof problem to solve before scaling.

questions.question: Which flavor actually sells best - Peach, Tropical, or the Variety Pack?
questions.listening_for: Confirms or breaks my assumption that the Variety Pack leads. If a single flavor wins, we change the lead product in the plan.

questions.question: Are people already searching for you by name, or searching things like 'hydration soda' and 'healthy soda' and finding you?
questions.listening_for: There is a real Google opening here. 'Healthy soda', 'best hydration drink', and rival brand names (Olipop, Poppi) get real search volume. If people search these, Google Search ads can catch buyers who are ready now, unlike Meta which interrupts. If nobody knows the 'hydration soda' term yet, we say so and hold off.

questions.question: Do you have a store locator and any retail stores carrying you? I noticed a store locator page.
questions.listening_for: If they are in stores, local search and 'where to buy' terms matter, and SEO can capture that. It also means brand searches are climbing, which strengthens the Google case.

objections.objection: We already run Meta ads ourselves, so what do you actually add?
objections.answer: You do, and that is a good start. But right now you have two near-duplicate ads splitting your budget against each other, and your ads claim zero sugar and zero calories while your site says honey and 45 calories. That gap costs you sales and could get flagged. We clean that up and test three real buyer angles properly so you know which one to put money behind, instead of guessing.

objections.objection: Our packs are $32.99. Can you really make paid ads pay off at that price?
objections.answer: Honest answer: that depends on your cost to make and ship a pack, which is why I asked. But $32.99 is a premium price, which helps. And your real weapon is the subscription at $16.50 first month - a first sale that turns into a repeat customer changes the math completely. We would test to prove it before you commit big spend.

objections.objection: We do not have many reviews, will that hurt the ads?
objections.answer: It makes cold traffic harder, yes, because people trust other buyers more than the brand. That is exactly why we would lead with your Variety Pack, which has the most reviews, and why we would pull any reviews you have off Amazon or email onto the site. Fixing your social proof is part of the plan, not a reason to wait.

objections.objection: I get ten of these pitches a day. Why you?
objections.answer: Because I already looked at your actual live ads and found real, specific problems - the duplicates and the sugar claim that fights your own site. Most people who email you have not opened your ad library. Give me 30 days on a small test budget and I will show you which angle works with your own numbers, not a template.

red_flags.flag: Ad claims contradict the product. Live ads say '0 sugar, 0 calories' but the product is honey-sweetened at 45 calories per can.
red_flags.why_it_matters: This confuses buyers, undercuts the honey story that makes them different, and false nutrition claims can get ads rejected or the account penalized. Must be raised gently and fixed early.

red_flags.flag: Almost no visible social proof. 2 reviews on Peach, 0 on Tropical, 14 on the Variety Pack, despite a '10,000+ Happy Customers' claim.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold shoppers trust other buyers. With little proof on the page, a $32.99 first order is a hard ask and conversion will suffer until we fix it.

red_flags.flag: High first-order price ($32.99) with no cheaper entry point except the subscription.
red_flags.why_it_matters: There is no low-cost trial pack. New buyers must commit $32.99 or sign up for a recurring charge. Both are friction. We need their margin to know if this can work profitably.

red_flags.flag: Very small catalog - three products, all the same price, only two flavors.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Limited room to test different products or price points, and repeat buyers may run out of flavors to try. Growth leans heavily on winning new customers and holding them on subscription.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads. Terms like 'healthy soda', 'hydration drink', and rival brand names (Olipop, Poppi) get real search traffic. These catch people who are already looking, which pairs well with Meta catching people who are not. Confirm on the call that people search the category before committing.

SEO around 'what is hydration soda' and 'best healthy soda'. They already have a Science page and a strong category story, so they can rank for education searches over time. This is a slower, longer play, not a 30 day fix, so frame it as a later phase.
